Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>

  <requested_at>2026-04-05</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

- Full user details (id, name, email, phone, address)
- Password hashes (bcrypt with $2b$12$ salt)
- Roles (all CUSTOMER)
- Creation/update timestamps
- Some have usernames (iaintest, mark_xaw2, dereckdale_2mla), others don't

This JSON export is likely the preparation step for importing into the new Render database. This is still discovery/preparation, but it's getting closer to the actual migration action.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Real user data exported from Neon in JSON format for migration</title>
  <subtitle>7 production customers extracted with full profiles including bcrypt password hashes and contact details</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Extracted 7 real customers excluding test accounts (westcoastmarine.local and example.com domains filtered out)</fact>
    <fact>User data includes bcrypt password hashes with $2b$12$ salt rounds</fact>
    <fact>User schema contains id, name, username, email, phone, address, passwordHash, role, suspendedUntil, createdAt, updatedAt fields</fact>
    <fact>Some users have optional username field populated (iaintest, mark_xaw2, dereckdale_2mla) while others have null</fact>
    <fact>All exported users have CUSTOMER role with no suspensions (suspendedUntil is null)</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Exported production user data from Neon database in JSON format, ready for migration. The query filtered out internal test accounts, capturing only 7 real customers with complete profiles. Each user record includes bcrypt-hashed passwords (12 salt rounds) that can be directly transferred to the new database without re-hashing. The data shows users registered between March 7-29, 2026, with detailed contact information including UK addresses and phone numbers. The username field is optional in the schema, appearing only in later registrations. This JSON export format enables straightforward import into the new Render PostgreSQL database.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Vessel data exported: 12 boats linked to real customers</title>
  <subtitle>Production vessels include boat details with foreign key relationships to user accounts for migration</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Exported 12 vessels belonging to 7 real customers from Neon database</fact>
    <fact>Vessel schema contains id, userId, boatName, boatType, boatNumber, yardNumber, createdAt, updatedAt fields</fact>
    <fact>Multiple users own multiple vessels (Dolapo Nuga has 3, Mark has 2, Dereck Dale has 2)</fact>
    <fact>Vessel types include Searay 230 Sundancer, sailboat, jetski, trawler, Boston Whaler, and various power boats</fact>
    <fact>YardNumber is optional field (null for some vessels) while boatNumber is populated for all vessels</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Extracted vessel data from production Neon database with foreign key relationships preserved to user accounts. The query joined Vessel and User tables to ensure only vessels belonging to real customers (excluding test accounts) are exported. The data shows 12 vessels with some customers owning multiple boats - notably Dolapo Nuga with 3 vessels (surfer, fireball, sea stormer) and two customers with 2 vessels each. Vessel records include registration details (boat numbers, yard numbers) and metadata useful for marina operations. The userId foreign keys will need to be maintained or remapped during migration to the new Render database to preserve ownership relationships.</narrative>

    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

content.thinking:
Excellent! This is a significant step - they've created a migration API endpoint that will perform the actual data migration. This is a FEATURE implementation.

Key aspects:
1. Created a new Next.js API route at /api/admin/migrate-neon
2. Embeds all 7 users and 12 vessels as constants in the file
3. Protected by a secret header (x-migration-secret: wcm-migrate-2026-neon-to-render)
4. Uses Prisma's upsert to avoid duplicates (where id exists, skip; otherwise create)
5. Returns results with counts and errors
6. Marked as one-time use, to be removed after migration
7. Uses nodejs runtime

This is the implementation of the migration mechanism - the actual migration hasn't been executed yet, but the tool is now in place.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Migration API endpoint created for Neon to Render database transfer</title>
  <subtitle>One-time admin endpoint upserts 7 users and 12 vessels with secret-based authentication</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Created POST endpoint at /api/admin/migrate-neon/route.ts protected by x-migration-secret header</fact>
    <fact>Migration secret is wcm-migrate-2026-neon-to-render for authentication</fact>
    <fact>Endpoint embeds 7 production users and 12 vessels as TypeScript constants with full data including password hashes</fact>
    <fact>Uses Prisma upsert pattern to prevent duplicate insertions (idempotent migration)</fact>
    <fact>Returns JSON response with counts of migrated users, vessels, and any errors encountered</fact>
    <fact>Designed for single-use execution and marked for deletion after migration completes</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented a secure one-time migration API endpoint to transfer production data from Neon to the new Render database. The endpoint accepts POST requests authenticated with a shared secret header, preventing unauthorized access. All 7 real customer accounts and their 12 associated vessels are embedded directly in the code with complete data including bcrypt password hashes, CUID identifiers, and timestamps. The migration uses Prisma's upsert operation, making it safe to run multiple times without creating duplicates - if a record with the same ID exists, it's skipped. The endpoint returns detailed results including success counts and any errors for each record. This approach allows controlled, auditable migration execution by making a single authenticated HTTP request to the deployed Render application.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/app/api/admin/migrate-neon/route.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

Schema sync skipped.\\\");\\n    globalThis.globalForPrisma = globalThis.globalForPrisma || {};\\n    globalThis.globalForPrisma.syncDone = true;\\n    return;\\n  }\\n\\n  try {\\n    console.log(\\\"🔄 Syncing database schema on startup...\\\");\\n    execSync(\\\"npx prisma db push --skip-generate --accept-data-loss\\\", {\\n      stdio: \\\"pipe\\\",\\n      env: { ...process.env, PRISMA_SKIP_ENGINE_CHECK: \\\"1\\\" },\\n    });\\n    console.log(\\\"✅ Schema sync completed\\\");\\n  } catch (error: any) {\\n    console.error(\\\"⚠️  Schema sync warning:\\\", error.message);\\n    // Don't fail startup; let queries fail if tables are actually missing\\n  }\\n\\n  globalThis.globalForPrisma = globalThis.globalForPrisma || {};\\n  globalThis.globalForPrisma.syncDone = true;\\n}\\n\\n// Create a Postgres pool for the adapter\\nconst pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL });\\n\\nexport const db =\\n  globalThis.globalForPrisma?.prisma ??\\n  new PrismaClient({\\n    adapter: new PrismaPg(pool),\\n    log: process.env.NODE_ENV === \\\"development\\\" ? [] : [],\\n  });\\n\\n// Sync schema on first db import in production\\nif (process.env.NODE_ENV === \\\"production\\\" && !globalThis.globalForPrisma?.syncDone) {\\n  syncSchemaIfNeeded().catch((err) => {\\n    console.error(\\\"Fatal error during schema sync:\\\", err);\\n  });\\n}\\n\\n// Prevent multiple instances in dev\\nif (process.env.NODE_ENV !== \\\"production\\\") globalThis.globalForPrisma = { prisma: db };\\n\",\"numLines\":68,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":68}}"</outcome>

content.thinking:
They're fixing the migration script - correcting the Prisma import and usage. They found that the correct export from @/lib/db is `db`, not `getPrisma()`, so they're updating both the import and the usage in the code. They've also removed the line that tried to instantiate prisma = getPrisma().

This is a bugfix - fixing an incorrect import/export before deployment.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed Prisma client import in migration endpoint</title>
  <subtitle>Corrected import from getPrisma to db matching actual export from database module</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Changed import statement from getPrisma to db in migrate-neon route</fact>
    <fact>Removed incorrect prisma variable instantiation line</fact>
    <fact>Updated all Prisma client references from prisma.user to db.user and prisma.vessel to db.vessel</fact>
    <fact>Database module src/lib/db.ts exports PrismaClient instance as db, not getPrisma function</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed import error in the migration endpoint before deployment. The database module exports a pre-configured PrismaClient instance named `db` with PostgreSQL adapter configuration, but the migration route initially tried to import a non-existent `getPrisma` function. Corrected the import statement and updated all database client references throughout the POST handler to use the correct `db` export. This ensures the migration endpoint will successfully connect to the Render PostgreSQL database when invoked.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/lib/db.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/app/api/admin/migrate-neon/route.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

1. Initial request: Migrate database information of registered users from old system to new one on Render

2. Discovery phase:
   - Checked local .env for old DATABASE_URL
   - Listed tables in local database (found User, Booking, Vessel, DailyMessage, PasswordReset tables)
   - Queried local User table (found 2 test users)

   - Found the production Neon database URL in .netlify/functions-internal directory
   - Connected to production Neon database and found 12 real users + 17 vessels
   - Exported 7 real users (excluding test accounts) as JSON
   - Exported 12 vessels belonging to those users as JSON

3. Implementation:
   - Created migration API endpoint at /api/admin/migrate-neon/route.ts
   - Embedded all user and vessel data as constants
   - Protected with secret header authentication
   - Used Prisma upsert for idempotent migration
   - Fixed import bug (getPrisma → db)
   - Committed and pushed to GitHub
   - Started polling for Render deployment to go live

4. Current state:
   - Migration endpoint is being deployed to Render
   - Background polling task running to check deployment status
   - Once live, will execute migration and verify data

The primary session is waiting for deployment to complete, then will execute the migration.

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Migrate registered users and vessels from old Neon database to new Render PostgreSQL deployment</request>
  <investigated>Investigated old system database structure by connecting to local dev database (2 test users) and production Neon database (12 real users, 17 vessels, 0 bookings). Searched for Neon connection strings across environment files and found production credentials in .netlify/functions-internal directory. Examined database schema showing User, Vessel, Booking, DailyMessage, and PasswordReset tables with Prisma management.</investigated>
  <learned>Old production system ran on Vercel with Neon PostgreSQL database containing 7 real customers (plus test accounts) created March 7-29, 2026, with 12 associated vessels. Users have bcrypt password hashes ($2b$12$ salt rounds), CUID identifiers, and optional username fields. Vessels have foreign key relationships to users via userId. Previous deployment had DATABASE_URL configuration issues on Vercel. Database uses Prisma with PostgreSQL adapter and connection pooling.</learned>
  <completed>Created and deployed migration API endpoint at /api/admin/migrate-neon/route.ts that embeds all 7 production users and 12 vessels as TypeScript constants. Endpoint uses secret-based authentication (x-migration-secret header) and Prisma upsert operations for idempotent migration. Fixed Prisma import bug (corrected from getPrisma to db export). Committed changes to GitHub and triggered Render deployment. Background polling task initiated to monitor deployment status.</completed>
  <next_steps>Waiting for Render deployment to complete (background poll running). Once deployment is live, will execute migration by calling the endpoint with authentication header. Will verify migrated data in Render database, then delete the one-time migration endpoint and push cleanup commit.</next_steps>
  <notes>Migration approach uses embedded data constants rather than live Neon connection to avoid runtime dependencies and ensure clean one-time execution. Upsert pattern prevents duplicate records if migration is run multiple times. All password hashes preserved for seamless user authentication on new system.</notes>
</summary>

content.thinking:
Ah! The build failure is due to a Prisma migration issue. The error is:

```
Error: P3009

migrate found failed migrations in the target database, new migrations will not be applied. Read more about how to resolve migration issues in a production database: https://pris.ly/d/migrate-resolve
The `20260405000000_init` migration started at 2026-04-05 22:56:05.777973 UTC failed
```

This is a problem - there's a failed migration in the database from an earlier deployment attempt. Prisma won't apply new migrations when there's a failed one. This needs to be resolved before the deployment can succeed.

This is a discovery about what's causing the build failure.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Build failure caused by failed Prisma migration in production database</title>
  <subtitle>Migration 20260405000000_init failed at 22:56:05 UTC, blocking new deployments with P3009 error</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Prisma migrate deploy command encountered P3009 error indicating failed migration in target database</fact>
    <fact>Migration 20260405000000_init started at 2026-04-05 22:56:05.777973 UTC and failed</fact>
    <fact>Failed migration blocks new migrations from being applied to production database</fact>
    <fact>Build command sequence is: npm install → npx prisma migrate deploy → npm run build</fact>
    <fact>Database connection confirmed to westcoast_marine_parks on dpg-d799o4v5r7bs73fnqgi0-a</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The Render deployment build fails during the Prisma migration step because a previous migration attempt left the database in a failed state. The 20260405000000_init migration started earlier in the evening (22:56 UTC) but did not complete successfully. Prisma's safety mechanism prevents applying new migrations when failed ones exist, requiring manual intervention to resolve the migration state. The build successfully completes npm install and Prisma client generation, but halts at the migration deploy step. To proceed with deployment and eventually run the data migration, the failed migration must be resolved - either by fixing and reapplying it, marking it as resolved, or rolling back to a clean state.</narrative>

    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Updated Render build command to resolve failed Prisma migration</title>
  <subtitle>Modified build pipeline to mark 20260405000000_init migration as applied before deploying new migrations</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Updated Render service build command via API to include prisma migrate resolve step</fact>
    <fact>New build command: npm install && npx prisma migrate resolve --applied 20260405000000_init || true && npx prisma migrate deploy && npm run build</fact>
    <fact>Added || true flag to prevent resolve command from blocking build if already resolved</fact>
    <fact>Resolve command marks failed migration as applied, allowing subsequent migrations to proceed</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed the deployment blocker by updating the Render service build command to handle the failed migration state. The updated pipeline now runs `prisma migrate resolve --applied` to mark the problematic 20260405000000_init migration as successfully applied before attempting to deploy any new migrations. The `|| true` ensures the build continues even if the migration is already resolved or the resolve command encounters non-critical errors. This approach addresses Prisma's P3009 error by manually correcting the migration state, allowing the deployment to proceed through the migration step and complete the build successfully.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>

<summary>
  <request>Resolve Render deployment failure and retry migration endpoint deployment</request>
  <investigated>Diagnosed build failure by fetching Render deployment logs. Found Prisma P3009 error indicating failed migration 20260405000000_init from earlier deployment at 22:56:05 UTC. Investigated Render API structure for updating service configuration, discovering build command must be nested under serviceDetails.envSpecificDetails for web services.</investigated>
  <learned>Prisma's migration safety mechanism blocks new deployments when failed migrations exist in the database. The P3009 error requires manual resolution using `prisma migrate resolve --applied` command to mark failed migrations as complete. Render API uses nested JSON structure for service configuration, with build commands requiring correct path: serviceDetails.envSpecificDetails.buildCommand. Failed migrations can be automatically resolved in build pipeline by adding resolve command with || true fallback.</learned>
  <completed>Fixed deployment blocker by updating Render service build command via API to include migration resolution step. New build pipeline: npm install → prisma migrate resolve (for failed migration) → prisma migrate deploy → npm run build. Successfully configured service and triggered new deployment (dep-d79eraggjchc73fjmtt0). Background polling task initiated to monitor deployment status.</completed>
  <next_steps>Monitoring new deployment build progress. Once deployment succeeds and goes live, will execute migration endpoint with secret authentication header to transfer 7 users and 12 vessels from Neon to Render database. Will then verify migrated data and clean up by deleting the one-time migration endpoint.</next_steps>
  <notes>The failed migration issue occurred during an earlier deployment attempt (approximately 20 minutes prior), leaving the production database in a blocking state. The fix is now permanent in the build command, preventing future migration failures from blocking deployments. This approach is safer than manual database intervention for resolving migration states.</notes>
</summary>

  <parameters>"{\"command\":\"git add src/lib/db.ts && git commit -m \\\"$(cat <<'EOF'\\nfix: remove blocking prisma db push from startup in db.ts\\n\\nsyncSchemaIfNeeded() ran npx prisma db push synchronously on cold start,\\nblocking the health check for ~65 seconds. Render's health check timed\\nout, keeping the deploy stuck in update_in_progress indefinitely.\\n\\nSchema is now managed exclusively by migrate deploy in the build command.\\n\\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>\\nEOF\\n)\\\" && git push origin main\",\"description\":\"Commit and push db.ts fix\"}"</parameters>
  <outcome>"{\"stdout\":\"[main d231b0d] fix: remove blocking prisma db push from startup in db.ts\\n 1 file changed, 26 insertions(+), 67 deletions(-)\\nTo https://github.com/nuga09/westcoast-marine-parks.git\\n   dc52091..d231b0d  main -> main\",\"stderr\":\"\",\"interrupted\":false,\"isImage\":false,\"noOutputExpected\":false}"</outcome>

content.thinking:
This is significant! They removed the automatic schema sync functionality from the db.ts file. The old version had a syncSchemaIfNeeded() function that ran `prisma db push` on startup in production, but this has been completely removed.

This makes sense because:
1. They're now handling migrations properly in the build command with `prisma migrate deploy`
2. The automatic sync was probably causing issues or conflicts
3. It's cleaner to manage migrations through the proper migration system rather than auto-sync on every startup

This is a refactor/change - removing unnecessary automation in favor of proper migration management.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>refactor</type>
  <title>Removed automatic schema sync from database module</title>
  <subtitle>Eliminated runtime schema synchronization in favor of build-time migration deployment</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Removed syncSchemaIfNeeded function that executed prisma db push on production startup</fact>
    <fact>Removed execSync import from child_process module</fact>
    <fact>Removed syncDone tracking from globalForPrisma type declaration</fact>
    <fact>Deleted production-only schema sync invocation and error handling</fact>
    <fact>Database module now only initializes PrismaClient with PostgreSQL adapter, no migration logic</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Simplified the database module by removing automatic schema synchronization that previously ran on every production startup. The old implementation executed `npx prisma db push --skip-generate --accept-data-loss` when the application started, tracking sync state globally to prevent duplicate runs. This approach is now superseded by proper migration deployment in the build pipeline via `npx prisma migrate deploy`, which runs once during deployment rather than on every instance startup. Removing the runtime sync logic eliminates potential race conditions, reduces startup time, and separates migration concerns from application runtime. The Prisma client initialization remains unchanged, still using the PostgreSQL adapter with connection pooling.</narrative>

  <investigated>Diagnosed deployment stuck in update_in_progress phase by testing health endpoint response time (65 seconds). Identified that Render's health check timeout was preventing deployment from completing because the application took too long to respond.</investigated>
  <learned>Runtime schema synchronization on startup causes deployment hangs when health checks timeout. The syncSchemaIfNeeded() function executed `npx prisma db push --skip-generate --accept-data-loss` synchronously during application initialization, blocking the event loop for 65+ seconds while waiting for database operations. Render's deployment system requires timely health check responses to mark deployments as live. Proper migration management belongs in build pipeline (migrate deploy) rather than runtime (db push on startup), separating concerns and eliminating cold start penalties.</learned>

Initiated new deployment with background monitoring to verify fix resolves timeout issue.</completed>

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Complete production database migration from Neon to Render with 7 users and 12 vessels</request>
  <investigated>Explored old system databases including local development (2 test users) and production Neon PostgreSQL (12 total users, 7 real customers). Examined database schema with User, Vessel, Booking, DailyMessage, and PasswordReset tables. Diagnosed two deployment blockers: P3009 failed migration error and health check timeout caused by blocking schema sync. Traced health endpoint responding in 65 seconds due to synchronous prisma db push on application startup.</investigated>
  <learned>Production data migration requires careful handling of password hashes (bcrypt $2b$12$), CUID identifiers, and foreign key relationships. Prisma's migration system blocks deployments when failed migrations exist (P3009 error), requiring explicit resolution via `migrate resolve --applied`. Runtime schema synchronization causes deployment hangs by blocking health checks - migrations belong in build pipeline, not application startup. One-time migration endpoints should use upsert patterns for idempotency and be immediately removed after execution for security. Render's free tier health checks require fast response times to complete deployments.</learned>
  <completed>Successfully migrated all production data from Neon to Render PostgreSQL: 7 customer accounts (Jay Faj, Dolapo Nuga, Iain Benady, Anthony Dale, Iain Test, Mark, Dereck Dale) and 12 vessels with complete profiles, password hashes, and ownership relationships preserved. Fixed deployment pipeline by adding migration resolution to build command and removing blocking schema sync from db.ts. Created, executed, and removed secure one-time migration endpoint. Zero migration errors or data loss. All users can authenticate with existing passwords on new Render deployment.</completed>
  <next_steps>Cleanup commit (bb2bbd5) removing migration endpoint is deploying. Migration work is complete - Render database now contains all production user data from old Neon system.</next_steps>
  <notes>Migration excluded test accounts (westcoastmarine.local domains and MassTest entries), transferring only real customer data. Three deployment attempts were required: first failed on migration error, second stuck on health check timeout, third succeeded after both fixes. The migration pattern (embedded data + upsert + immediate cleanup) proved effective for small-scale data transfers without runtime database connections.</notes>
</summary>
